['The Sister viewers fume they’ve ‘wasted four hours’ after each episode gets ‘more stupid than the last’ THE SISTER viewers have been left fuming that they’ve “wasted four hours” as the finale drops after each episode gets “more stupid than the last”.

After weeks of hype and anticipation, the series starring Russell Tovey, Bertie Carvel and Amrita Acharia has left some fans feeling underwhelmed.

Tonight saw the finale of ITV’s The Sister, based on the book The Burial by Luthur creator Neil Cross.

And to say some fans were left disappointed would be an understatement. Taking to Twitter viewers voiced their thoughts and feelings on the supposedly creepy and sinister four-part series.

One wrote: “This series is dragging hope there is a really good ending otherwise it’s hrs of my life I won’t get back #TheSister”

“I’m not gonna lie, I am not blown away by this. Expected to be a lot more gripped! Hoping the ending will be a massive twist! #thesister,” added another.

While a third ranted: “Normally these things have a stupid ending, this has been stupid from the start and keeps being stupid.#thesister”

The series began promisingly with a nail-biting and sinister opening sequence that saw Tovey’s character Nathan about to swallow a handful of pills with a neat pint of vodka.

However, after he’s stopped in his tracks by a press conference from missing woman Elise Fox’s family.

Then, all of a sudden, the episode jumps forward seven years into the future and Nathan is actually married to Elise’s sister Holly [Amrita Acharia].

Last night the third instalment of the show aired and fans couldn’t help but draw comparisons between the show and The Sixth Sense thinking there would be a big twist and the characters were all ghosts.

The Sister, the brainchild of Luther creator Neil Cross and based on his book The Burial, tells the story about what really happened that fateful night 10 years ago when two men buried a body in the haunted woods.

Although the events of the novel are fictional, the author has revealed how a moment from his life inspired his book.

At a press event for the new ITV drama, Neil said as a teenager he believed he killed someone.', 'Will there be a series 2 of The Sister? ITV'S The Sister has already raked in rave reviews.

While some viewers complained about the cast's "mumbling", many are eager to know if there will be a second series.

ITV hasn't confirmed whether there will be a second series of the spooky drama.

The Sister's writer, Neil Cross, has based the series on his book titled Burial.

If series one of The Sister ends in line with the book, this could mean it's a stand-alone show - but fans will have to watch this space.

The fact it's such a short series too suggests it will end here.

The book is based on a nightmare Neil had in 1985 about murdering a homeless man.

He said when he awoke, the dream felt so vivid that he worried it could have been real.

He said: "I wanna say even now I hope I dreamt it. I’m pretty sure [it was a dream] – I was very young, 16 or 17, and I was out of my head on rough cider in Bristol."

He added: "I was making my way back to my sister’s house, and there was a short cut… through the woods, through these concrete steps through the woods.

"And I just woke up the next morning not with any sense that I’d had a dream, of any description, I woke up with a very, very clear memory of coming across a homeless man asleep on the steps who I randomly stabbed to death."

Warning - spoilers!

In the final episode of series one, Nathan confronts Bob about the secrets they share regarding Elise's death and burial in the forest.

Meanwhile the show explores further the relationship between Nathan and Holly and how they mourn Elise given that Holly believes Elise is lost, while Nathan knows she's dead.

Things don't sit right with Jacki, who then sets out to find out the truth about what happened to Elise for Holly's sake.

Bob's real intentions about why he returned to Nathan after all this time emerge, which causes Nathan to fight back.

In Burial, the book that's inspired the show, it's revealed that Bob and Nathan took turns to have sex with Elise before she died.

Fans can catch up on missed episodes on ITV Hub.

Series one was made up of four episodes, aired over consecutive nights.', "The Sister ending explained: The meaning behind the finale of the ITV drama series The Sister finally came to a conslusion last night but many viewers were left feeling a bit unsure about the ending.

The creepy ITV drama had people guessing as what was going on the whole way through, but now the fourt-parter has come to an end some say they are “disappointed”.

The show, starring Russell Tovey, followed the story of married man with a dark secret that had come back to haunt him.

Here we try to unpack the ending, and figure out what it all meant as well as sharing what viewers thought of it.

Nathan (Tovey), is a man who has managed to keep his involvement with a woman named Elise’s death several years ago a secret.

Elise happened to be the sister of the woman who is now his wife, Holly (Amrita Acharia).

Nathan thought Elise’s death was an accident, but he later learns from his acquaintance Bob (Bertie Carvel) that this was not the case.

In fact, Bob had caused her death by spiking cocaine she had taken with cyanide, which he had originally intended for Nathan to take as well.

It was part of a plan to create a ghost, as he has believed a haunting would happen in the wood if she had died there that night, based on ancient traditions.

The plan did not seem to work, but Elise had been haunting Bob ever since.

Bob tried to convince Nathan that they have to come clean about Elise’s death and return her body to her family for a proper burial, as he thought that was the only way to make the haunting stop.

But Nathan was not so keen on the idea – he was married to Elise’s sister after all.

Instead, he got the police to suspect Bob, rather than him, by changing his statement about what had happened the night Elise died, speaking to detective Jacki.

Once he had got the police on side, he set out to kill Bob but did not quite manage this.

He put sleeping pills in Bob’s whisky and then tried to finish the job with a pillow.

But rather than dying, Bob was left in a coma, unable to defend himself or grass up Nathan.

Eventually Elise’s skull is found in Bob’s flat, and detective Jacki revealed that though she knew Nathan had killed Bob, she was not going to say anything.

The show jumps forward, at least nine months, as when we next see Nathan and Holly, they have a baby daughter. This is significant as they had had fertility issues in the past.

But it is not quite the happy ending you might initially think, as when Nathan is driving his car, he sees something scary in the rearview mirror.

Elise is there, unmoving in his back seat, with her eyes wide open.

Perhaps she really is going to haunt him forever.

The Sister cast member Amrita Acharia told Radio Times that the ending was intended to be “ambiguous” and there were several ways it could have gone.

“That ending – talk about an open end. I found it really creepy [the ending] because I think there were a few different versions of it, I think there was a version with a [car] crash. I loved it because it’s ambiguous”, Acharia told the outlet.

She also mused that the fact that Holly and Nathan finally had a baby at the end after dealing with fertility issues, could have meant that there meant that a curse or haunting placed on them by Elise had been broken.

“You sort of feel like the haunting is over because they’ve had this baby, but then [there’s] this plot twist at the end, where she’s in the back of the car, so maybe it’s just not enough, or maybe she’s angry at someone else, who knows?” she told Radio Times.

The show has received mixed reviews from viewers, with many not very satisfied with the ending.

Many have taken to social media to vent their frustration over the ending.

Some said they were “disappointed” while others branded the ending “a bit daft.”

What a rubbish ending to #TheSister ….. very disappointed!— steven deakin (@deakinkoz)

I'm thinking of starting a petition to make sure they can't make a second series of #TheSister— Peter Green (@Greeno1967)

Am I the only one not confused by #TheSister ? Or have I missed the point of it?— Soraya Jane Kashfi (@sorayakashfi)

Quite a nightmarish ending to #TheSister, with a happy one before that. A gripping drama these four nights brought to us by a brilliant cast. Really enjoyed it overall.— Daniel Brown (@DanJBrownMUFC)

One user went as far as quipping about starting a petition to ensure they do not make another series.

Meanwhile some were just left a bit confused by it all.

“Am I the only one not confused by The Sister? Or have I missed the point of it?” one Twitter user said.

Some people did enjoy the show, however.

Another viewer said: “Anyone looking for a Halloween binge watch this weekend cannot recommend The Sister on ITV highly enough! Edge of seat, jaw on floor, insane acting.”

The Sister is available to stream on ITV Hub now.

The i on TV newsletter is a daily email full of suggestions of what to watch as well as the latest TV news, opinions and interviews. Sign up here to stay up to date with the best new TV."]

Name: 35_je_gaitskill_ai_qu
Representative_Docs:
['Lost Cat by Mary Gaitskill, review: a fierce emotional punch When novelist Mary Gaitskill was asked if she could help to look after a litter of feral kittens while on a writing retreat in Italy, she initially declined. Gaitskill, best known for her short story collection Bad Behaviour, liked cats but didn’t want the distraction. A week later, she was persuaded to visit them and fell in love with a soft grey tabby with a bulging belly, protruding bones and a gluey eye. She took him to a vet and then kept him in her flat, where she watched his fear turn to affection. She called him Gattino and took him back home to live with her and her husband, Peter, in America.

The title of Gaitskill’s essay Lost Cat, which first appeared in the literary magazine Granta, gives away what happened next. After 10 weeks, Gattino went missing. Gaitskill distributed flyers, knocked on doors, left food in the garden in case he returned, and even visited a psychic. But she doesn’t shrink from the inevitable. “He was very young, at seven months barely an adolescent,” she writes. “He is probably dead, but I don’t know for certain.”

Gaitskill’s desolation unearths buried feelings about her relationship with two children, Caesar and Natalia, whom she fostered several years earlier. They would spend summers and occasional weekends with her and Peter. The pair had been born into difficult circumstances – their father had left them and their mother beat and manipulated them.

Both were prone to self-destructive behaviour and were often hostile to Gaitskill’s attempts to bring calm and warmth into their lives. Had she failed the kitten, she wonders, as well as failing two children?

Gaitskill’s fiction, though lauded, has often been described as cold, and in Lost Cat there is a bluntness to her language that could easily be read as detachment; I flinched when, after an argument with Caesar, she observes “my feelings for him went dead”. Nonetheless, the book delivers a fierce emotional punch. Gaitskill is unsentimental yet insightful about the complexities and contradictions of human emotion.

“Who decides which relationships are appropriate and which are not?” she asks. “Which deaths are tragic and which are not? Who decides what is big and what is little?”

The House by the Lake: children’s books are tackling complex topics from the Holocaust to the gender pay gap

She analyses her complicated feelings towards those close to her; as well as the two children, she examines her relationship with her late father, whom she helped to care for when he was dying, and her two sisters. Her book is about the ways we try to bestow love and the ways others accept it – or not. Gaitskill tried to make her foster children feel loved, but experience had taught them to keep her at arm’s length. Gattino accepted her affection but in the end went his own way.

To love and be loved, the book suggests, we must look at our expectations and motivations. The workings of the heart, says Gaitskill, “can never be fully seen or understood”. But that doesn’t stop us trying.

Lost Cat by Mary Gaitskill (Daunt Books, £8.99)', "James Rebanks: 'We have destroyed things on a scale our ancestors wouldn't have believed' James Rebanks describes himself as a chameleon, jumping between the roles of farmer and writer. His latest book, English Pastoral: An Inheritance, showcases his talents in both roles and is an impassioned and superbly written argument for making agriculture more biodiverse and sustainable, a philosophy he is putting to tangible use on his Lake District farm.

Following on from his wildly successful debut, The Shepherd’s Life, this second book is about inheritance and the natural world, starting with the love for nature that his grandfather instilled in him and ending with the legacy he wishes to leave to his children. In between, Rebanks builds an insider’s picture of where farming has gone wrong and issues a powerful wake-up call to the public, a plea to act now alongside farmers to stem the harmful impact that pesticides and other modern agricultural techniques continue to have on our ecosystems.

Rebanks’ grandfather was the person who first inspired him to take in and prize his surroundings. “He knew where the foxes went under the wire fences, he knew where the badgers lived. He knew where the buzzard’s nest was, just across from where I am now. He devoted an enormous amount of time to just looking at things. Taking time to take in the world around him.”

It is clear from our conversation and from English Pastoral that Rebanks has wholeheartedly inherited his grandfather’s sharp eye for nature: our Zoom call is punctuated by comments on scenes taking place outside his office window, including two kestrels having a fight with a carrion crow. Just like his book, the effect is to make you wish you were there on the farm with him.

Equally palpable is just how rooted Rebanks is to his family’s land, which his father and grandfather worked before him. As well as making improvements to his own fields, woodland, hedgerows and becks for future generations, he is obsessed by the beauty of the surrounding landscape. Last week, he told his 135,000 Twitter followers that he and others in the community had clubbed together to buy and “breathe new life” into one of the last remaining dale-head Lakeland farms, located at the top of their valley. They were outbid, leaving him “heartbroken”.

Rebanks’ passion for sustainable farming came relatively recently. “If you had asked us [him and his father] 15 or 20 years ago, we would have said this was a really nature-friendly farm already,” he says, before proudly listing some of the progress that has been made in just a handful of years under his stewardship.

Sales of tell-all autobiographies from celebrities are falling as Britons opt for juicy political memoirs

Bird and flower species are rapidly increasing thanks to new wetland restoration and grazing techniques, while the soil is growing richer in the absence of chemicals. “We have seen little egrets, green sandpipers and endless other water-based birds that we’ve never seen before on the farm. Nobody in this valley had ever seen a little egret,” he says, with characteristic enthusiasm.

Rebanks knew that farming had been led astray decades ago when he read Rachel Carson’s Silent Spring, a landmark book on the environmental damage caused by pesticides. Later, he saw the benefit of sustainable living while travelling to world heritage sites for Unesco. The final piece of the jigsaw slotted into place when he visited the American Midwest. As Rebanks explains: “I’d heard lots of suspicions, I’d read lots of books. I was aware what people were saying was wrong with modern farming, but until you’ve really seen it and met the people in it, you haven’t completed the journey.”

To him, what he witnessed in Iowa was farming taken to its “logical conclusion”, a system in which farmers are cut off from society and beholden to large corporations solely driven by profit.

Unsurprisingly, he finds abhorrent the possibility of a free-trade deal which will allow US products such as chlorinated chicken into the British market. “I think it would be the greatest disaster for the British countryside of anything that has happened in the last 200 years,” he says.

To combat this threat, Rebanks would like to see an “army of citizens” clamouring for a different future, one which forces politicians to prioritise environmental concerns instead of over-farming.

“Our power to beat up Mother Nature has grown exponentially in my lifetime, wearing the mask of progress. And because of that we have destroyed things on a scale our ancestors would scarcely have believed,” Rebanks writes in one passage. His book is a passionate plea to make it stop.

English Pastoral: An Inheritance by James Rebanks is out now (Allen Lane, £20)

“It’s about some nutty guy who tried to go up Everest and tried to fly in there with a biplane, which is not my usual reading matter – but Ed is such an amazing writer.”", 'Le pochoir, tout un art, par C215 L’artiste urbain sort un nouveau livre qui explique les techniques du pochoir. Astucieusement, Christian Guémy fait appel aux témoignages d’une quinzaine de spécialistes du « street art », permettant de retracer l’histoire et d’offrir un panorama de cet art particulier.

Christian Guémy, alias C215, publie un nouveau livre, superbement illustré, très réussi, Le Manuel du pochoir (Eyrolle), dans lequel il dresse un inventaire des techniques sur l’art du pochoir, un terme qui désigne un outil, un procédé et l’œuvre qui en résulte.

« J’ai ressenti ma première émotion face à un pochoir en 2004, au détour d’une ruelle de Barcelone. Il s’agissait d’un pochoir du duo d’artistes américains Faile, commence Christian Guémy en guise d’introduction, face à une reproduction de l’œuvre qu’il évoque. Ma curiosité pour le pochoir était née »… jusqu’à ce qu’il devienne « pochoiriste professionnel en 2007 ».

Aujourd’hui, pour réaliser ses pochoirs, il explique qu’il « découpe directement au scalpel dans du papier. Je travaille à partir d’impressions photographiques non retouchées. Celles-ci me servent de gabarits pour conserver les mêmes proportions d’un pochoir à l’autre. Je crée des pochoirs allant d’une à plusieurs couches. Je les scanne et les archive, car ces découpes originales sont fragiles et de petite taille. Ensuite, je les agrandis dans du carton kraft »… Avant de citer quelques artistes qui l’inspirent, qui ne sont pas nécessairement des pochoiristes : Swoon, Ernest-Pignon Ernest, Ben Vautier… et Le Caravage !…

L’idée de ce livre ? Il n’a pas voulu « inculquer à d’autres une pratique personnelle », mais s’adresser à un « public [qui] a souvent une vision extrêmement réduite du pochoir ».

Et donc naturellement, si l’iconographie fait appel à des œuvres de Nemo, Obey, ou Banksy, C215 s’est adressé à « de nombreux artistes [qui] ont accepté de se prêter au jeu de l’interview », de divers horizons, livrant au fil des pages leur savoir-faire, émaillant les chapitres méthodologiques (« Matériel et outils », « Les notions de base », « La création du pochoir », « Peindre au pochoir », « Les principaux sujets »). Florilège.

M-City : « C’est la multiplication qui m’importe. Je considère que c’est l’essence des pochoirs ».

Stinkfish : « Mon processus commence par une photographie (…). A partir des photos sélectionnées, je réalise un travail numérique avec Photoshop (…). Je projette le résultat final à la taille souhaitée (…). J’utilise généralement du papier léger, bon marché, facile à découper et à transporter. Une fois l’image projetée sur le papier, je la découpe en essayant de conserver des ponts suffisamment large pour avoir une structure de pochoir résistante (…). J’utilise un seul layer de pochoir. Je peins ensuite les détails en couleur de mes portraits à la peinture aérosol ».

Nick Walker : « Je n’aime pas vraiment être présenté comme un artiste pochoiriste. Si vous choisissez de faire quelque chose, c’est toujours pour la liberté. L’art, c’est la liberté, tout simplement ».

Speedy Graphito : « Je me suis tourné vers le pochoir quand j’ai commencé à vouloir peindre dans la rue. Cette technique permettait une exécution répétitive de mes images. A cette époque, je venais de finir mon apprentissage en école d’art. Mes peintures n’intéressaient pas les galeries parisiennes. J’ai donc eu l’idée de les reproduire au pochoir de manière simplifiée et de les exposer dans la rue ».

Jef Aérossol : « J’ai débuté à la fin des années 1970 (…). J’ai eu plusieurs jobs de gardien de nuit dans des usines, des bureaux ou des entreprises. C’est là que j’ai commencé à m’amuser avec les photocopieuses ! (…) A l’automne 1982, après avoir quitté Nantes pour Tours, je suis passé à l’acte. A partir d’un autoportrait photomaton agrandi à la photocopieuse, j’ai découpé mon premier pochoir et je l’ai bombé sur les murs du vieux Tours (…). Une nuit, je me suis mis à glisser le pochoir tout en bombant et j’ai compris que j’avais trouvé le moyen simple de reproduire sur les murs les effets que la photocopieuse me permettait. Ma technique du pochoir glissé était née ».

Miss.Tic : « Avec l’utilisation de l’informatique et des logiciels de dessin, les pochoirs se rapprochent de plus en plus de la photographie et de la technique de l’aérographe avec plusieurs pochoirs (…). mes deux premières influences ont été Ernest-Pignon Ernest et Gérard Zlotykamien ».

Epsylon Point : « Je suis un puriste : pour moi, un pochoir c’est un seul layer (…). Ce qui me branche, c’est de dessiner les formes. Les découpes, c’est casse-pieds (…). Peu à peu, tous les étudiants des écoles d’art sont arrivés avec leurs ordinateurs et ils ont commencé à faire des multilayer sans se soucier de la contrainte de temps, propre à la penture dans la rue. Et ils font tous la même chose. Je ne parle pas de ceux qui découpent à la main, c’est autre chose ».

Logan Hicks : « Les pochoirs sont le miroir de ce que je pense (…). Je vois les éléments en couches : je peux observer le bien et le mal, le facile et le complexe, le laid et le beau en même temps. Les pochoirs comportent tellement de couches différentes que cela me permet de traiter toutes les pensées qui accompagnent ma création. J’ai l’impression que chaque peinture que je produis correspond à quatre œuvres d’art à cause de la somme de réflexions que je consacre à chaque couche »…

On termine avec C215 lui-même, qui explique son souhait de « faire corps avec les supports », ses pochoirs ayant « une esthétique tribale », utilisant le mobilier urbain dans sa diversité : murs, bien sûr, mais aussi armoires électriques ou boîtes aux lettres. Très présente, aussi, sa fille : « Je souhaitais peindre dans la rue des portraits de Nina non loin des lieux qu’elle pouvait fréquenter »…

Enfin, pour permettre de passer de la théorie à la pratique, l’auteur propose, en fin d’ouvrage, trois pochoirs détachables cartonnés, parmi lesquels le portrait de sa chienne Lili.']
